chunks.page_summary: This page provides an integration guide for connecting Rheonics analog signal outputs (4-20 mA) from their viscometers and density sensors to an Allen Bradley Micro820 PLC, detailing required firmware, software versions, and wiring instructions.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Analog signal Integration with Micro820

Modified on: Sun, 15 Feb, 2026 at 12:50 AM

1. Getting started

1.1. Rheonics SRV, SRD, DVP or DVM w/ Firmware V03.20/0 or higher
1.2. Allen Bradley Micro820 2080-LC20-20QBB
1.3. Rockwell Automation CCW Version 12.00.00 (includes RSLinx Classic Lite 4.11.00 CPR 9 SR 11.0)
1.4. Windows 10 64 bit
1.5. 500 Ω Terminal resistance
1.6. RheonicsSRV_micro820_analoginput.ccwarc

2. PLC Wiring

2.1. Connect SME 4-20 mA output channels to the PLC analog inputs by using a resistance connected to the analog input's channels (For micro820 those go from I0 to I03, the resistance is needed because the PLC only read voltage from 0 to 10V). For this example, we are using an impedance of 500Ω.
key_facts: The document details the analog signal integration of Rheonics SRV, SRD, DVP, or DVM sensors with an Allen Bradley Micro820 PLC.
Required Rheonics sensor firmware is V03.20/0 or higher.
The specific Allen Bradley Micro820 model for this integration is 2080-LC20-20QBB.
Rockwell Automation CCW Version 12.00.00, including RSLinx Classic Lite 4.11.00 CPR 9 SR 11.0, is required.
The system operates on Windows 10 64-bit.
A 500 Ω terminal resistance is specified for the analog signal integration.
The file RheonicsSRV_micro820_analoginput.ccwarc is related to the setup.
SME 4-20 mA output channels should be connected to the PLC analog inputs (I0 to I03 for Micro820).
A resistance is necessary for the analog input connection because the PLC only reads voltage from 0 to 10V.
An impedance of 500Ω is used for the example wiring.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for wiring analog inputs into a Micro820, including a voltage-current correlation table and a note on powering SMET channels.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 1. Analog inputs in Micro820

2.2. Follow this table to fix the correct values for 4095 point available for the analog input and the correlation with the resistance.

| Voltage[V] | Current[mA] | Voltage[V] | Current[mA] |
|------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| 2          | 4.00        | 6.5        | 13.00       |
| 2.5        | 5.00        | 7          | 14.00       |
| 3          | 6.00        | 7.5        | 15.00       |
| 3.5        | 7.00        | 8          | 16.00       |
| 4          | 8.00        | 8.5        | 17.00       |
| 4.5        | 9.00        | 9          | 18.00       |
| 5          | 10.00       | 9.5        | 19.00       |
| 5.5        | 11.00       | 10         | 20.00       |
| 6          | 12.00       |            |             |

Table 1. Voltage-Current Correlation

2.3. Wire the channels from the SME according to the article(https://support.rheonics.com/support/solutions/articles/81000297457-connecting-4-20-ma-outputs). All the channels from the SMET are already internally powered so no additional power supply is need it
key_facts: The document describes configuring analog inputs for a Micro820.
A table provides a voltage-current correlation for the 4095 analog input points.
SME channels should be wired by following a specific Rheonics support article.
SMET channels are internally powered and do not require an external power supply.
The image displays a wiring terminal block with specific input labels and a resistance value.
warnings_notes: All the channels from the SMET are already internally powered so no additional power supply is need it.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions and screenshots for configuring 4-20mA output settings and performing calibration checks on a Rheonics control panel, including system and software information.
chunks.extracted_text:

key_facts: The Rheonics control panel allows users to configure the 4-20mA output channels by setting upper and lower limits and assigning specific measurement parameters.
The control panel displays system information, including Electronics S/N and Interface FW Version.
Three channels are shown for 4-20mA settings, pre-configured for Viscosity (mPa.s), Density (g/cc), and Temperature (°C) respectively.
A 4-20mA Calibration Check section provides live mA readings for each channel and allows for calibration and testing at 10 mA.
Density settings for SRV models can be configured as a constant value or a temperature-dependent curve.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions on how to select measurement parameters and configure the 4-20 mA output limits for various parameters like viscosity, density, and temperature using a software interface.
chunks.extracted_text:
3.2. Open the dropdown list and select the parameter to measure. The parameters available are:
* Viscosity
* Density
* Temperature
* Kinematic Viscosity
* Calculated Viscosity
* Calculated Density
* Concentration

4-20 mA Settings

Figure 4. Selecting parameter in dropdown list

3.3. To change the upper and lower limits, follow the next steps:
2.3.1. Enter the lower limit on the box labeled “4 mA”.
2.3.2. Write the upper limit on the box labeled “20 mA”.
2.3.3. Click the “Update” button to send the new values to the SME. If the write operation succeeds, the “Write” button will turn green for a few seconds. Otherwise, will turn red and the boxes will go back to the previous values.
key_facts: Users can select from parameters such as Viscosity, Density, Temperature, Kinematic Viscosity, Calculated Viscosity, Calculated Density, and Concentration for measurement.
The 4-20 mA output range can be configured by setting lower (4 mA) and upper (20 mA) limits for each channel.
Changes to the limits are applied by clicking the 'Update' button, which sends new values to the SME.
A successful write operation to the SME is indicated by the 'Write' button turning green for a few seconds.
An unsuccessful write operation to the SME is indicated by the 'Write' button turning red, and the input boxes reverting to their previous values.
warnings_notes: If the write operation succeeds, the “Write” button will turn green for a few seconds. Otherwise, will turn red and the boxes will go back to the previous values.

4. PLC setup
This section explains how to navigate in the CCW software to configure the correct analog inputs settings. Rheonics provides a premade .CCWARC as a basic program for the analog input connection.
1. Open the Connected Component Workbench (CCW) Application
key_facts: The 4-20 mA settings screen allows configuration of analog output ranges for viscosity, density, and temperature measurements.
Rheonics provides a pre-made .CCWARC program for basic analog input connection.
The setup process begins by opening the Connected Component Workbench (CCW) Application.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for launching Connected Components Workbench and importing a specific Rheonics project file named 'RheonicsSRV_micro820_analoginput.ccwarc'.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 6. Connected Components Workbench start
2. Once in the CCW environment go to the “file” tab and select Import project.
Figure 7. Import Project in CCW
3. Choose the file named “RheonicsSRV_micro820_analoginput.ccwarc”. All the project configurations will be displayed on the screen.
key_facts: The process starts by launching 'Connected Components Workbench'.
Users must navigate to the 'File' tab within the CCW environment.
The 'Import Project' option needs to be selected from the 'File' menu.
The specific project file to be chosen is 'RheonicsSRV_micro820_analoginput.ccwarc'.
Selecting this file will display all project configurations on the screen.
diagram_labels: Design, configure, program, visualize and maintain

key_facts: A `.ccwarc` file named "RheonicsSRV_micro820_analoginput.ccwarc" can be imported into the Connected Components Workbench software.
Analog input settings for the Micro820 controller can be verified by selecting the "Global Variable" option in the software's Project Organizer.
The software displays a table of I/O variables (e.g., IO_EM_DO_xx, IO_EM_DI_xx), with all listed variables having a "BOOL" data type.
Limits for the ladder program's analog inputs are configured using values consistent with those found in the RCP software.
diagram_labels: RheonicsSRV_analoginput - Connected Components Workbench Standard Edition
Figure 9. Checking analog inputs on Micro820.

key_facts: The page demonstrates ladder logic for processing analog inputs (viscosity and density) in a Micro820 PLC.
The Micro820 PLC program uses `ANY_TO_REAL` and `SCALER` blocks to convert and scale analog input signals.
The `SCALER_6` and `SCALER_4` blocks define the output limits (0.0 to 3000.0) used in the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) for viscosity and density, respectively.
Output values from the PLC and RCP can be improved by calibrating in the "Service" tab of the Rheonics Control Panel.
The Rheonics Control Panel displays real-time measured values for viscosity, density, temperature, and pressure.
The control panel also shows the status (Running, Logging, Pressure mode) and a historical plot of viscosity and density over time.
warnings_notes: These values can be improved by calibrating in "Service" tab
